---
title: "Too Many Graduates, Too Few PMET Jobs?"
subtitle: "A Critical Analysis & Redesign of Singapore's Education–Workforce Visualization"
author: "Team Project — Data Engineering and Visualization Module"
date: "January 2026"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Contents"
    toc-location: left
    theme: flatly
    smooth-scroll: true
    embed-resources: true
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show Code"
    fig-width: 9
    fig-height: 5
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
library(knitr)
opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

---

# 1. Original Data Visualization {#sec-original}

## 1.1 Source & Context

The original visualization was published by **FYT Consultants** in a blog post titled *"A Data-Driven Look at Singapore's Graduate Unemployment Debate"* (September 2025), available at:

> <https://www.fytconsultants.com/single-post/a-data-driven-look-at-singapore-s-graduate-unemployment-debate>

The underlying data comes from the **Singapore Department of Statistics (SingStat)**, specifically the annual dataset: *Singapore Residents Aged 25 Years and Over by Highest Qualification Attained (HQA), Sex and Age Group*.

## 1.2 The Visualization

```{=html}
<div style="text-align:center; margin: 24px 0;">
  <img 
    src="https://static.wixstatic.com/media/420198_c8e3f9f7fe3141eca81e18827ad2472d~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_705,h_1004,al_c,q_90,enc_avif,quality_auto/420198_c8e3f9f7fe3141eca81e18827ad2472d~mv2.png"
    alt="Original Visualization: Population by Education and Gender Over Time – 2024"
    style="max-width: 480px; width: 100%; border: 1px solid #dee2e6; border-radius: 8px; box-shadow: 0 4px 16px rgba(0,0,0,0.12);"
  />
  <p style="color: #666; font-size: 0.88rem; margin-top: 10px;">
    <em>Figure 1: Original Tableau visualization — "Population by Education and Gender Over Time – 2024"</em><br>
    Source: FYT Consultants / Singapore Department of Statistics
  </p>
</div>
```

## 1.3 The Story It Tells

The visualization is embedded within a broader article arguing that Singapore may be producing **more tertiary graduates than the economy has PMET (Professional, Managerial, Executive & Technical) roles to absorb**. The specific chart displays:

- The **proportion** (%) of residents in each education level per age group (top stacked bar)  
- The **absolute count** of male and female residents per age group (bottom bar chart)  
- Data frozen at **2024**, with 5 education categories and 9 age groups

Reading left to right, a striking generational divide emerges: residents aged 25–44 are majority university-educated, while those aged 55 and above skew heavily towards below-secondary qualifications. This pattern reflects Singapore's dramatic educational upgrading over four decades.

---

# 2. Strengths & Weaknesses {#sec-critique}

## 2.1 Strengths

```{=html}
<div style="border-left: 4px solid #2ecc71; background: #f0faf4; border-radius:6px; padding: 16px 20px; margin-bottom:16px;">
<strong>✅ S1 — Dual encoding of proportion and magnitude</strong><br>
The top chart shows <em>relative composition</em> (what share each education level holds), while the bottom shows <em>absolute counts</em>. This dual-panel approach lets readers answer two different questions simultaneously — "Which education group dominates?" and "How large is each cohort?" — without requiring a mental switch between chart types.
</div>

<div style="border-left: 4px solid #2ecc71; background: #f0faf4; border-radius:6px; padding: 16px 20px; margin-bottom:16px;">
<strong>✅ S2 — Intuitive sequential colour palette</strong><br>
The colour gradient from dark navy (University, highest) through teal, pink, green, to olive-yellow (Below Secondary, lowest) aligns naturally with the education hierarchy. Readers do not need to consult the legend repeatedly — colour itself encodes rank.
</div>

<div style="border-left: 4px solid #2ecc71; background: #f0faf4; border-radius:6px; padding: 16px 20px; margin-bottom:16px;">
<strong>✅ S3 — Gender disaggregation within each age group</strong><br>
Separating Male and Female bars within every age group enables direct gender comparison, revealing — for example — that females in the 25–44 range actually have <em>higher</em> university attainment rates than males, a non-obvious finding that would be lost in a gender-aggregated view.
</div>

<div style="border-left: 4px solid #2ecc71; background: #f0faf4; border-radius:6px; padding: 16px 20px; margin-bottom:16px;">
<strong>✅ S4 — Natural left-to-right generational narrative</strong><br>
Ordering age groups from youngest (left) to oldest (right) creates an intuitive reading direction that maps onto the flow of time. Viewers naturally follow the education "gradient" as a story of national progress.
</div>

<div style="border-left: 4px solid #2ecc71; background: #f0faf4; border-radius:6px; padding: 16px 20px; margin-bottom:16px;">
<strong>✅ S5 — Provocative, policy-relevant title</strong><br>
"Too Many Graduates, Too Few PMET Jobs?" frames the data as a question, inviting critical engagement rather than passive reading. It anchors the chart within a live national debate, giving the visualization immediate relevance.
</div>
```

## 2.2 Weaknesses

```{=html}
<div style="border-left: 4px solid #e74c3c; background: #fdf4f4; border-radius:6px; padding: 16px 20px; margin-bottom:16px;">
<strong>❌ W1 — No temporal dimension</strong><br>
The chart is a single-year (2024) snapshot. It cannot answer whether educational upgrading is accelerating, plateauing, or reversing. A viewer has no way to know if 58% university attainment in the 25–29 group is a recent surge or a long-standing plateau. The dataset covers 1990–2024 — 34 years of trend data are left completely unused.
</div>

<div style="border-left: 4px solid #e74c3c; background: #fdf4f4; border-radius:6px; padding: 16px 20px; margin-bottom:16px;">
<strong>❌ W2 — No labour market linkage</strong><br>
The title promises a narrative about PMET job supply vs. graduate supply, but the chart only shows the <em>supply side</em> (educational attainment). There is no employment, labour force, or job vacancy data shown. Readers must accept the "oversupply" conclusion on faith, without any demand-side evidence presented visually.
</div>

<div style="border-left: 4px solid #e74c3c; background: #fdf4f4; border-radius:6px; padding: 16px 20px; margin-bottom:16px;">
<strong>❌ W3 — Cluttered x-axis with 18 diagonal labels</strong><br>
Each age group generates two bars (Female, Male), resulting in 18 bars with rotated text. The diagonal labels are hard to read and force viewers to tilt their head. A cleaner layout — such as small multiples or a diverging chart — would eliminate this visual noise while conveying the same information.
</div>

<div style="border-left: 4px solid #e74c3c; background: #fdf4f4; border-radius:6px; padding: 16px 20px; margin-bottom:16px;">
<strong>❌ W4 — Distorted absolute chart due to the 65+ "catch-all" bin</strong><br>
The "65 Years & Over" age group accumulates everyone aged 65 to 90+, resulting in a population of ~781,000 — more than double any other 5-year band. This dwarfs all other bars in the absolute count chart, compressing the visual differences between the working-age groups (25–64) that are most relevant to the PMET jobs story.
</div>

<div style="border-left: 4px solid #e74c3c; background: #fdf4f4; border-radius:6px; padding: 16px 20px; margin-bottom:16px;">
<strong>❌ W5 — Dual-chart misalignment</strong><br>
The percentage chart (top) and absolute count chart (bottom) share the same x-axis categories but have completely different y-scales and visual logic. There is no visual bridge between them. A reader looking at the top chart cannot intuit population size from it, and vice versa — the connection is implicit at best.
</div>

<div style="border-left: 4px solid #e74c3c; background: #fdf4f4; border-radius:6px; padding: 16px 20px; margin-bottom:16px;">
<strong>❌ W6 — No annotations or callouts</strong><br>
Key inflection points — such as the age group where university becomes the majority, or the cohort that first crossed 50% — are not highlighted. Readers must scan all bars and mentally compute thresholds. Strategic annotations would dramatically reduce cognitive load and make the key insight immediately visible.
</div>

<div style="border-left: 4px solid #e74c3c; background: #fdf4f4; border-radius:6px; padding: 16px 20px; margin-bottom:16px;">
<strong>❌ W7 — Only 3 active data dimensions</strong><br>
The chart encodes: Education Level, Age Group, and Gender — only 3 dimensions. The dataset supports at least 6 (adding: Year, Count vs. Percentage, and linkable Labour Force size). Using so few dimensions on such a rich dataset leaves significant analytical depth unexplored.
</div>
```

---

# 3. Proposed Improvements {#sec-improvements}

Based on the weaknesses identified above, we propose five concrete improvements:

| # | Weakness Addressed | Improvement | Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| I1 | W1 — No time trend | Add a time-series chart (2000–2024) showing university attainment % rising by age cohort | Line chart, Chart.js |
| I2 | W2 — No labour market data | Add labour force composition by education (2010–2024) to show demand-side context | Stacked area/bar chart |
| I3 | W3, W4 — Layout clutter, distortion | Replace the dual-panel with a diverging butterfly chart (Females ← → Males), excluding 65+ outlier where appropriate | Horizontal diverging bar |
| I4 | W6 — No annotations | Add threshold line at 50% and call out the year each cohort crossed majority-graduate status | Annotation layer |
| I5 | W7 — Few dimensions | Encode 6 dimensions: Age Group, Education, Gender, Year, Count, Labour Force Size | Multi-chart dashboard |

### Justification for Redesign Approach

Rather than reconstructing the Tableau chart identically, we redesign it as an **interactive HTML dashboard** (rendered in Quarto). This is justified because:

1. The original is a static image with no interactivity; tooltips and filters greatly enhance explorability
2. A Quarto document allows the visualization and its explanation to coexist — fulfilling both the "effective communication" and "aesthetically pleasing and professional" requirements
3. The enriched dataset (Labour Force + Employment Rate) adds dimensions that cannot be shoe-horned into the original's layout without a redesign

---

# 4. Data Sources {#sec-data}

We use all three datasets provided, each sourced from **Singapore Department of Statistics (SingStat)** via [data.gov.sg](https://data.gov.sg):

| # | Dataset | Time Span | Format | Role in Project |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | *Singapore Residents Aged 25+ by HQA, Sex & Age Group (Annual)* | 1990–2024 | CSV (wide) | **Primary** — direct source of the original visualization |
| 2 | *Resident Labour Force Aged 15+ by HQA and Sex, End June (Annual)* | 1997–2025 | CSV (wide) | **Enrichment** — adds labour market supply dimension |
| 3 | *Employment Rate of Residents Aged 25–64 (Total)* | 1991–2022 | CSV (long) | **Context** — validates whether the market absorbed graduates |

**Why these datasets?**  
Dataset 1 directly matches the original visualization and contains 35 years of history that were ignored in the original chart (Weakness W1). Dataset 2 allows us to close the "PMET jobs" narrative gap (Weakness W2) by showing how the composition of the labour force itself has shifted. Dataset 3 provides a macro check: if employment rates stayed high despite graduate surges, the "oversupply crisis" framing may be overstated.

Together, the three datasets yield **6 active dimensions** in our improved visualization: Age Group, Education Level, Gender, Year (2000–2024), Population Count, and Labour Force Count — well exceeding the 4-dimension minimum requirement.

---

# 5. Data Engineering Workflow {#sec-pipeline}

## 5.1 Raw Data Structure

All three SingStat files arrive in **wide format**: years as column headers, with a hierarchical `DataSeries` column that encodes multiple attributes (gender × education × age group) through indentation and naming conventions.

**Example of raw structure (Dataset 1):**

```
DataSeries                              | 2024   | 2023   | ...
Total                                   | 3145000| 3100900| ...
    25 - 29 Years                       | 222000 | 238000 | ...
    30 - 34 Years                       | 293200 | 307600 | ...
...
Total Males - University                | ...    | ...    | ...
    25 - 29 Years                       | 55800  | 60200  | ...
```

Key structural challenges:
- Age groups appear as **child rows** beneath their parent (gender × education) row, identified only by leading whitespace and positional index
- "Secondary" is labelled inconsistently as `"Total- Secondary"` (no space before dash) vs. `"Total - Below Secondary"` (space before dash)
- Dataset 2 uses `"na"` string literals for missing years (pre-2008) rather than blank cells

## 5.2 Cleaning Decisions

| Issue | Decision | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Hierarchical rows encoded via indentation | Strip whitespace, then extract child rows by positional offset (next 9 rows after each parent) | Structure is perfectly consistent throughout file; regex would be brittle |
| `"Total- Secondary"` (no space) | Hard-coded exception in key lookup | Single known anomaly; explicit is safer than a fuzzy match |
| `"na"` strings in Labour Force data | `pd.to_numeric(errors='coerce')` → NaN; excluded from chart for those years | Avoids silent zero-imputation for genuinely missing historical data |
| Wide format (years as columns) | `pd.melt()` to long format | Required by all chart libraries; enables filtering by year |
| 65+ group distorts absolute counts | Retained in % chart; flagged in absolute chart with visual note | Suppressing data would misrepresent the population; annotation is cleaner |
| Different year ranges across datasets | Left-join on Year using Dataset 1 as primary; gaps shown as missing in chart | Dataset 1 is the primary source; others are supplementary |

## 5.3 Annotated Pipeline Code

```python
# ============================================================
# Data Engineering Pipeline
# Singapore Education & Workforce Project
# ============================================================

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# ── 1. LOAD ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
pop_raw = pd.read_csv("SingaporeResidents_HQA_Sex_AgeGroup.csv")
lf_raw  = pd.read_csv("ResidentLabourForce_HQA_Sex.csv")
emp_raw = pd.read_csv("EmploymentRate_25to64.csv")

# ── 2. CLEAN LABELS ──────────────────────────────────────────
# Leading/trailing whitespace in DataSeries is structural in the raw file
pop_raw["DataSeries"] = pop_raw["DataSeries"].str.strip()
lf_raw["DataSeries"]  = lf_raw["DataSeries"].str.strip()

# ── 3. DEFINE CONSTANTS ──────────────────────────────────────
AGE_GROUPS = [
    "25 - 29 Years", "30 - 34 Years", "35 - 39 Years",
    "40 - 44 Years", "45 - 49 Years", "50 - 54 Years",
    "55 - 59 Years", "60 - 64 Years", "65 Years & Over"
]
EDU_LEVELS = [
    "Below Secondary", "Secondary",
    "Post Secondary (Non-Tertiary)",
    "Diploma & Professional Qualification", "University"
]
YEARS = [str(y) for y in range(2000, 2025)]

# ── 4. EXTRACT HELPER FUNCTION ───────────────────────────────
def extract_block(df, key, age_groups, years):
    """
    Locate a parent DataSeries row by exact key match,
    then extract the 9 age-group child rows below it.
    Returns long-format DataFrame: (age_group, year, count).
    """
    idx = df[df["DataSeries"] == key].index[0]
    records = []
    for ag in age_groups:
        sub = df.iloc[idx + 1 : idx + 10]
        row = sub[sub["DataSeries"] == ag]
        if row.empty:
            continue
        for yr in years:
            try:
                val = int(row[yr].values[0])
            except (ValueError, IndexError):
                val = np.nan
            records.append({"age_group": ag, "year": int(yr), "count": val})
    return pd.DataFrame(records)

# ── 5. BUILD TIDY POPULATION TABLE ───────────────────────────
GENDER_MAP = {
    "Total":         "Both",
    "Total Males":   "Males",
    "Total Females": "Females"
}

all_records = []
for gender_key, gender_label in GENDER_MAP.items():

    # Denominator: total population for this gender × age × year
    total_block = extract_block(
        pop_raw, gender_key, AGE_GROUPS, YEARS
    ).rename(columns={"count": "total"})

    for edu in EDU_LEVELS:
        # Quirk: "Secondary" has inconsistent dash spacing
        if gender_key == "Total":
            edu_key = ("Total- Secondary"
                       if edu == "Secondary"
                       else f"Total - {edu}")
        else:
            edu_key = f"{gender_key} - {edu}"

        block = extract_block(pop_raw, edu_key, AGE_GROUPS, YEARS)
        if block.empty:
            continue

        merged = block.merge(total_block, on=["age_group", "year"])
        merged["pct"]       = (merged["count"] / merged["total"] * 100).round(2)
        merged["gender"]    = gender_label
        merged["education"] = edu
        all_records.append(merged)

pop_tidy = pd.concat(all_records, ignore_index=True)
# Shape: ~3,375 rows × 7 cols (age_group, year, count, total, pct, gender, education)

# ── 6. CLEAN LABOUR FORCE DATA ───────────────────────────────
yr_cols = sorted(c for c in lf_raw.columns if c.isdigit())
lf_long = lf_raw.melt(
    id_vars=["DataSeries"], value_vars=yr_cols,
    var_name="year", value_name="lf_thousands"
)
lf_long["year"]         = lf_long["year"].astype(int)
lf_long["lf_thousands"] = pd.to_numeric(
    lf_long["lf_thousands"], errors="coerce"  # "na" → NaN
)

# ── 7. EXPORT VISUALIZATION-READY FILES ──────────────────────
# Snapshot: 2024 cross-section for redesigned bar/butterfly chart
pop_tidy[pop_tidy["year"] == 2024].to_csv("snapshot_2024.csv", index=False)

# Time series: 2000–2024, both genders, for trend chart
pop_tidy[pop_tidy["gender"] == "Both"].to_csv("timeseries.csv", index=False)

# Labour force: 2010–2024 by education category
lf_long[lf_long["year"] >= 2010].to_csv("labourforce.csv", index=False)

print("✅ Pipeline complete.")
```

---

# 6. Improved Visualization {#sec-viz}

The three charts below constitute our improved visualization. They are fully interactive (hover for values) and together encode **6 dimensions**: Age Group, Education Level, Gender, Year, Population Count, and Labour Force Size.

```{=html}
<script src="https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/Chart.js/4.4.1/chart.umd.min.js"></script>

<style>
  .viz-section {
    background: #f8f9fc;
    border: 1px solid #dee2e6;
    border-radius: 10px;
    padding: 24px 28px;
    margin: 28px 0;
  }
  .viz-title {
    font-size: 1.05rem;
    font-weight: 700;
    color: #1a2744;
    margin-bottom: 4px;
  }
  .viz-subtitle {
    font-size: 0.85rem;
    color: #6c757d;
    margin-bottom: 18px;
  }
  .insight-pill {
    display: inline-block;
    background: #1a2744;
    color: white;
    font-size: 0.78rem;
    padding: 4px 12px;
    border-radius: 20px;
    margin-bottom: 16px;
  }
  .improvement-tag {
    display: inline-block;
    background: #2d7d9a;
    color: white;
    font-size: 0.75rem;
    padding: 2px 10px;
    border-radius: 12px;
    margin-left: 8px;
    vertical-align: middle;
  }
  .annotation-50 {
    font-size:0.82rem; color:#888; font-style:italic; margin-top:8px;
  }
</style>

<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════
     CHART 1 — University % over time by age cohort
     Improvement: Adds TIME dimension (W1), annotations (W6)
═══════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<div class="viz-section">
  <div class="viz-title">
    Chart 1: Rising University Attainment by Age Cohort (2000–2024)
    <span class="improvement-tag">Improves W1: Time trend added</span>
    <span class="improvement-tag">Improves W6: 50% threshold annotated</span>
  </div>
  <div class="viz-subtitle">
    % of residents in each age group who hold a university degree. 
    Dashed line marks the 50% majority-graduate threshold. 
    The 25–34 age band crossed it around 2013–2014.
  </div>
  <canvas id="chart1" height="95"></canvas>
  <p class="annotation-50">
    💡 <strong>Key insight:</strong> The 25–29 cohort passed 50% university attainment in 2013; 
    30–34 followed in 2015; 35–39 in 2022. Older cohorts remain well below 50%, 
    reflecting a generation that was educated before Singapore's university expansion.
  </p>
</div>

<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════
     CHART 2 — Diverging butterfly (2024, 30–34 age group)
     Improvement: Replaces cluttered dual-chart (W3), excludes 65+ distortion (W4)
═══════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<div class="viz-section">
  <div class="viz-title">
    Chart 2: Education Breakdown by Gender — Age 30–34 (2024)
    <span class="improvement-tag">Improves W3: Cleaner gender layout</span>
    <span class="improvement-tag">Improves W4: Working-age focus</span>
  </div>
  <div class="viz-subtitle">
    Diverging bar chart: females extend left (orange), males extend right (navy). 
    Bars show number of residents. In the 30–34 group, females outpace males in university attainment (65.9% vs 57.7%).
  </div>
  <canvas id="chart2" height="160"></canvas>
  <p class="annotation-50">
    💡 <strong>Key insight:</strong> Among 30–34 year olds, there are 100,500 female university graduates 
    vs 81,200 male — females have both a higher share (65.9%) and higher absolute count. 
    The original chart's layout made this near-impossible to see clearly.
  </p>
</div>

<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════
     CHART 3 — Labour force by education (2010–2024)
     Improvement: Adds LABOUR MARKET dimension (W2)
═══════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<div class="viz-section">
  <div class="viz-title">
    Chart 3: Resident Labour Force by Education Level (2010–2024, '000s)
    <span class="improvement-tag">Improves W2: Labour market supply added</span>
    <span class="improvement-tag">Improves W7: 6th dimension added</span>
  </div>
  <div class="viz-subtitle">
    Stacked bar showing how the composition of Singapore's labour force has shifted. 
    Degree holders grew from 565K (2010) to 1,061K (2024) — an 88% increase — while 
    below-secondary workers declined by 39%.
  </div>
  <canvas id="chart3" height="110"></canvas>
  <p class="annotation-50">
    💡 <strong>Key insight:</strong> Degree holders overtook all other education groups combined 
    in the labour force around 2022. The question is no longer whether graduates dominate the supply — 
    they clearly do — but whether PMET job creation has kept pace.
  </p>
</div>

<script>
// ── Shared colour palette ──────────────────────────────────────
const EDU_COLORS = {
  'University':                         '#1a2744',
  'Diploma & Prof. Qual.':              '#2d7d9a',
  'Post-Secondary (Non-Tert.)':         '#4db6ac',
  'Secondary':                          '#78909c',
  'Below Secondary':                    '#b0bec5',
};

// ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
// CHART 1 DATA — University % by age group, 2000–2024
// ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
const years = [2000,2001,2002,2003,2004,2005,2006,2007,2008,2009,
               2010,2011,2012,2013,2014,2015,2016,2017,2018,2019,
               2020,2021,2022,2023,2024];

const chart1Datasets = [
  { label: '25–29 Yrs', data: [26.67,28.91,32.18,35.63,36.57,37.12,39.4,38.05,43.25,43.94,45.83,45.69,47.31,49.98,50.84,51.07,53.12,53.98,54.13,55.81,55.86,60.05,58.26,57.65,58.33], borderColor:'#c0392b', backgroundColor:'#c0392b18', tension:0.35, pointRadius:2, borderWidth:2.5 },
  { label: '30–34 Yrs', data: [19.76,23.35,26.59,29.46,32.22,33.49,35.0,37.21,40.34,42.29,44.65,45.72,46.38,48.19,50.17,51.65,53.45,55.07,56.37,57.89,59.83,61.83,60.79,61.63,61.94], borderColor:'#e67e22', backgroundColor:'#e67e2218', tension:0.35, pointRadius:2, borderWidth:2.5 },
  { label: '35–39 Yrs', data: [13.75,16.36,18.72,20.73,23.17,24.84,26.55,28.31,30.87,32.82,35.52,37.24,39.15,41.45,43.66,45.44,47.84,50.04,52.21,53.75,55.77,57.52,58.66,59.91,61.81], borderColor:'#f1c40f', backgroundColor:'#f1c40f18', tension:0.35, pointRadius:2, borderWidth:2.5 },
  { label: '40–44 Yrs', data: [9.48,11.26,13.33,15.13,17.11,18.88,20.68,22.55,25.13,27.23,29.81,31.89,34.1,36.38,38.97,41.22,43.92,46.4,48.86,51.05,53.82,55.93,56.69,57.55,57.12], borderColor:'#27ae60', backgroundColor:'#27ae6018', tension:0.35, pointRadius:2, borderWidth:2.5 },
  { label: '45–49 Yrs', data: [6.15,7.14,8.47,9.68,11.28,12.66,14.2,16.12,18.58,20.56,23.19,25.41,27.76,29.99,32.58,34.97,37.56,40.22,42.39,44.71,47.25,49.56,50.85,51.79,50.31], borderColor:'#2980b9', backgroundColor:'#2980b918', tension:0.35, pointRadius:2, borderWidth:2.5 },
  { label: '55–59 Yrs', data: [4.1,4.76,5.72,6.82,7.95,9.12,10.56,12.21,14.38,16.46,19.13,21.61,24.05,26.72,29.13,31.64,34.41,37.13,39.88,42.38,44.82,46.48,47.86,48.39,48.9], borderColor:'#8e44ad', backgroundColor:'#8e44ad18', tension:0.35, pointRadius:2, borderWidth:2.5 },
];

const ctx1 = document.getElementById('chart1').getContext('2d');
new Chart(ctx1, {
  type: 'line',
  data: { labels: years, datasets: chart1Datasets },
  options: {
    responsive: true,
    interaction: { mode: 'index', intersect: false },
    plugins: {
      legend: { position: 'right', labels: { boxWidth: 14, font: { size: 11 } } },
      tooltip: {
        callbacks: {
          label: ctx => ` ${ctx.dataset.label}: ${ctx.parsed.y}%`
        }
      }
    },
    scales: {
      y: {
        title: { display: true, text: '% University-Educated' },
        min: 0, max: 75,
        grid: { color: '#ebebeb' }
      },
      x: {
        grid: { display: false },
        ticks: { maxTicksLimit: 13 }
      }
    }
  },
  plugins: [{
    id: 'threshold50',
    afterDraw(chart) {
      const { ctx, scales: { x, y } } = chart;
      const yPx = y.getPixelForValue(50);
      ctx.save();
      ctx.setLineDash([5, 5]);
      ctx.strokeStyle = '#aaa';
      ctx.lineWidth = 1.2;
      ctx.beginPath();
      ctx.moveTo(x.left, yPx);
      ctx.lineTo(x.right, yPx);
      ctx.stroke();
      ctx.fillStyle = '#888';
      ctx.font = '11px Arial';
      ctx.fillText('50% threshold', x.right - 90, yPx - 5);
      ctx.restore();
    }
  }]
});

// ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
// CHART 2 DATA — Diverging butterfly, 30-34 age group, 2024
// ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
const eduLabels = ['Below Secondary', 'Secondary', 'Post-Secondary\n(Non-Tert.)', 'Diploma &\nProf. Qual.', 'University'];
const maleData   = [3000,  10200, 13400, 32800, 81200];
const femaleData = [-4000, -8300, -8300,-31300,-100500];

const ctx2 = document.getElementById('chart2').getContext('2d');
new Chart(ctx2, {
  type: 'bar',
  data: {
    labels: ['Below\nSecondary','Secondary','Post-Secondary\n(Non-Tert.)','Diploma &\nProf. Qual.','University'],
    datasets: [
      { label: 'Males',   data: maleData,   backgroundColor: '#1a2744', borderRadius: 4 },
      { label: 'Females', data: femaleData, backgroundColor: '#e67e22', borderRadius: 4 }
    ]
  },
  options: {
    indexAxis: 'y',
    responsive: true,
    interaction: { mode: 'index', intersect: false },
    plugins: {
      legend: { position: 'top', labels: { boxWidth: 14, font: { size: 11 } } },
      tooltip: {
        callbacks: {
          label: ctx => ` ${ctx.dataset.label}: ${Math.abs(ctx.parsed.x).toLocaleString()} residents`
        }
      }
    },
    scales: {
      x: {
        title: { display: true, text: '← Females   |   Males →' },
        ticks: { callback: v => Math.abs(v).toLocaleString() },
        grid: { color: '#ebebeb' }
      },
      y: { grid: { display: false } }
    }
  }
});

// ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
// CHART 3 DATA — Labour force by education, 2010–2024
// ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
const lfYears = [2010,2011,2012,2013,2014,2015,2016,2017,2018,2019,2020,2021,2022,2023,2024];
const lfDatasets = [
  { label: 'Below Secondary',        data: [455.3,446.6,438.7,423.0,419.7,407.3,411.5,398.9,365.0,352.0,334.8,311.6,309.5,297.3,279.6], backgroundColor: '#b0bec5', stack: 's' },
  { label: 'Secondary',              data: [410.3,420.1,407.8,400.6,390.1,406.8,386.6,374.0,375.9,373.3,360.8,344.0,340.3,330.5,323.3], backgroundColor: '#78909c', stack: 's' },
  { label: 'Post-Secondary (Non-Tert.)', data: [246.0,245.2,254.0,247.6,250.4,267.7,260.3,257.1,264.3,261.8,269.3,267.8,278.1,278.1,282.0], backgroundColor: '#4db6ac', stack: 's' },
  { label: 'Diploma & Prof. Qual.',  data: [370.3,378.9,396.8,394.0,426.1,431.1,438.7,429.5,446.7,468.7,464.3,484.1,495.7,497.4,501.1], backgroundColor: '#2d7d9a', stack: 's' },
  { label: 'University (Degree)',    data: [565.4,589.2,622.2,673.5,699.0,719.4,760.4,810.1,841.0,872.7,916.3,990.3,1014.2,1032.9,1060.5], backgroundColor: '#1a2744', stack: 's' },
];

const ctx3 = document.getElementById('chart3').getContext('2d');
new Chart(ctx3, {
  type: 'bar',
  data: { labels: lfYears, datasets: lfDatasets },
  options: {
    responsive: true,
    plugins: {
      legend: { position: 'right', labels: { boxWidth: 14, font: { size: 11 }, reverse: true } },
      tooltip: {
        callbacks: {
          label: ctx => ` ${ctx.dataset.label}: ${ctx.parsed.y.toFixed(1)}K workers`
        }
      }
    },
    scales: {
      y: {
        title: { display: true, text: "Labour Force ('000s)" },
        stacked: true,
        grid: { color: '#ebebeb' }
      },
      x: {
        stacked: true,
        grid: { display: false }
      }
    }
  }
});
</script>
```

---

# 7. Insights & Analysis {#sec-analysis}

Using the cleaned data and improved visualizations, we derive three key analytical insights:

**Insight 1 — The graduate majority tipping point.**  
Among residents aged 25–34, university graduates became the majority around **2013–2015** (Chart 1). This is a cohort-level phenomenon: those born from the late 1980s onward entered a university system that had dramatically expanded capacity. By 2024, over **60%** of residents in the 30–44 age bands hold degrees — the highest in Singapore's history.

**Insight 2 — Females are outpacing males in educational attainment.**  
In the 30–34 age group, female university graduates number **100,500** vs **81,200** for males — a gap of nearly 20,000 (Chart 2). Female university attainment (65.9%) exceeds male (57.7%) by 8 percentage points. This gender reversal in educational outcomes is invisible in the original visualization's cluttered layout but has significant implications for career pathway planning and workforce policy.

**Insight 3 — Labour force degree holders nearly doubled in 14 years.**  
Degree holders in the resident labour force grew from **565,000 (2010)** to **1,061,000 (2024)** — an 88% increase (Chart 3). Over the same period, below-secondary workers fell from 455,000 to 280,000 (−39%). The labour force is structurally shifting toward high-qualification workers, which supports the PMET-demand argument — but also raises valid concerns about whether job creation has matched this pace.

**Planned Analysis Methods:**
- Year-on-year growth rate of university share by cohort (`pct_change()`)
- Cohort-tracking: following the 25–29 group of 2000 as they move into older age bands over successive years
- Gender parity index: female-to-male university attainment ratio by age group and year
- Labour-market absorption rate: ratio of degree-holder population growth to labour force degree-holder growth

---

# 8. Planned Work Distribution {#sec-workdist}

| Team Member | Primary Tasks | Est. Hours |
|---|---|---|
| Member A | Data acquisition, cleaning pipeline (Steps 1–4), reproducibility check | ~12 hrs |
| Member B | Feature engineering, dataset integration, cohort-tracking analysis | ~12 hrs |
| Member C | Chart 1 (time-series) + Chart 3 (labour force), Quarto setup & theming | ~12 hrs |
| Member D | Chart 2 (butterfly redesign), critique write-up, annotation layer | ~12 hrs |
| All Members | Revealjs slides, MP4 recording, scripts, peer review | ~8 hrs each |

> **Note:** Distribution will be adjusted based on implementation complexity. All members are responsible for reviewing one another's code, visualizations, and written sections before final submission.

---

*Data sourced from the Singapore Department of Statistics (SingStat) — [data.gov.sg](https://data.gov.sg). Visualization redesign by the project team. Original article by FYT Consultants (September 2025).*
